chapters.title: The chicken, the egg, and the big life question
chapters.text:
Have you ever paused to ask yourself what you are really doing with your life. Maybe you were getting ready for a job you hate, or squeezing through a smoky city crowd when you would rather grow grapes at a French villa. Many of us push that question into the drawer of unanswerable ones, alongside when did time begin or the chicken and the egg puzzle. But why.

Aesop fable of the Ant and the Grasshopper makes the contrast clear. The Ant worked hard all summer to survive the winter, while the Grasshopper enjoyed life. Although we are all born Grasshoppers, most of us end up becoming Ants. No wonder so many people feel miserable. Working is fine for survival, but life has more to it than that.

Working feeds your body, but enjoying life feeds your soul.

It is easy to get lost in pages of self-help books full of advice. The key, though, is to balance work with play.

In this summary, you will learn ways to enjoy life regardless of your bank account. You will see how to focus on abundance rather than lack, and how even the smallest things and adventures can bring you joy, fulfillment, and peace. Do not just exist. Thrive.

chapters.title: Stories that quietly steal your life
chapters.text:
Although life can be a game, for most people it is a struggle. You work to buy stuff, and in doing so, you waste your life at work. It is a vicious circle that not everyone can escape. It does not have to be so gloomy, though, when you ask yourself why you need that stuff in the first place.

Time is the one investment you never get back.

Many people fall victim to the stories they tell themselves, and those stories end up leading them to the wrong life choices. Some of those stories are these. Believing you have plenty of time, which is a trap most people never see. They keep putting off family vacations or a hobby because there is always a more pressing issue. Never asking yourself what you really want, which is easy to lose track of when you are busy with endless to-do lists and responsibilities. Waiting until retirement to live your dream, which is the belief that retirement is the only time to have fun, travel, and connect with peers. Indeed, that time can be beautiful, but your arthritis will not let you climb to Machu Picchu if that is your dream, and your heart will not appreciate the adrenaline rush on a roller coaster. Any experience you want to live through needs the right timing. Rejecting fun in order to save more, where financial smarts only help if they do not pull you into a dull, joyless routine. Some people forget they earn money to live. Instead, they live to make money. The fear of staying penniless pushes some to sacrifice the most important thing of all, which is time.

Sadly, many people still live on autopilot, doing what they do without a particular purpose. Some wait for the right time to go after what they always wanted, and that time never arrives. Then, they end up sitting in a chair, tired and unwilling to leave it. You can escape this route by choosing to die with zero, to save but not at the cost of your own life. This way, you may not leave millions to your children, but you will leave them with millions of memories.

chapters.title: What those new boots really cost you
chapters.text:
Time is money, but not in the way you usually think. The average person spends at least eight hours a day earning a set amount. Later, they trade that money for goods and services. So, if you make $20 an hour and the boots you want cost $100, you will have to work five hours to buy them. That number may not sound scary, but we buy more than boots. We pay for food, medication, clothes, TV, vacations, and hobbies, and all of it costs us our time. Even if you earn $200 an hour and the boots cost $5,000, you are still paying for them with your life.

Invest in your life experiences, and start early, start early, start early.

That does not mean you should not work. It means you should spend wisely. New boots may bring pleasure for a few days, but will you remember them in ten or twenty years. On the other hand, you will probably remember camping with your kids, playing board games with friends, or a spontaneous trip to Europe. Spending in that way becomes an investment, not a waste.

Earn money to thrive, not to buy.

So, what can you do to make the most of your time. Ask yourself which experiences you want in your life. They can be small, like eating McDonald in a parking lot. Knowing is halfway to doing. Save when you have more, and spend when you have less, since your salary is not fixed for life. Who said you cannot earn a million five years from now. The rule of thumb for an eager saver who misses out on life is to take care of your present, and your future will take care of itself. Check yourself before paying for things, since wasting too much stops you from experiencing life. Invest money into your memories, not into toys.

chapters.title: Dying with zero is not the same as dying broke
chapters.text:
Many people keep piling up resources for better or worse times, and in doing so, forget to enjoy them. They skip their lives in constant expectation.

Granted, money has absolutely no value to you when you are dead, which is why I say you should die with zero.

Dying with zero is the idea of spending as much money as possible on experiences and not waiting until retirement to enjoy life. Of course, this approach is not perfect. You cannot die with exactly zero in your bank account, because you will either run out of money before you die or die with too much too early. The first option terrifies people, since no one wants to die on the streets. There are some possible solutions worth looking into. Buy income annuities that will provide for you if you outlive your savings. You purchase a set sum from an insurance company and make a deal that a specific amount will be paid to you monthly, no matter how long you live. Become your own life insurer by calculating and setting aside the amount you will need for the rest of your life.

Spend on your life, not on an imaginary retirement.

Dying with zero puts many people off, because they believe they must earn as much as possible to leave something to their children. Although that practice is common, it is illogical for a couple of reasons. Your legacy is more than money. Many people put work ahead of time with their kids because they have to provide for the family. However, most kids would rather have experiences you give them, like a family trip or your presence at the recital, than a few hundred more in their inheritance check. Give money when they need it, not when you are dead. Most people inherit between 50 and 60, which makes it a nice bonus to their savings rather than a meaningful gift. The best time to give money to your children is between 26 and 35, since they are still healthy and active enough to make the most of it. That way, you give them the chance at a more memorable and outstanding life.

chapters.title: Do not skip life, you only get one
chapters.text:
Everyone life is different, but they all follow the same seasons, from toddler to teen to adult. Somewhere in the middle, they reach their peak, and their health starts to slip. Many people realize they missed something or turned in the wrong direction long after they can fix it. For example, some regret missing their children toddler years because of long work hours. Others feel sorry for staying in the same place their whole life. However, no one regrets taking an extra vacation.

Have you ever watched The Bucket List, where two elderly men go skydiving, drive vintage cars, and do other wild things to fulfill their dreams before they kick the bucket. How much more satisfying would those experiences be if the men were healthier and younger.

No matter how old you are, you are not getting any younger. Now is the best moment.

The key to happiness is to make the most of each life season. So, if you want to live with no regrets, create your own time buckets. Divide the timeline of your life into groups of five to ten years. Draw a large bucket for each group. On a separate sheet of paper, write down every experience and how many times you want it. Do not think about money, because financial worries will stop you from dreaming big. You may not see how you can afford something now, but that does not mean you will not be able to afford it in two years. Then, think about the age at which you would like to experience these things and place them in the right buckets. You will not go bungee jumping when you are 60, and skiing at 75 is not for most people, but these adventures fit well in your twenties or thirties.

A quick note. According to a Forbes Health article from September 2023, a midlife crisis is a period in a person life marked by deep anxiety and a sharp shift in perspective that comes from realizing their own mortality.

chapters.title: Gambling on your own happiness
chapters.text:
Any dream needs courage, and you must be willing to give up what you have in order to earn more. Sometimes, picking a path feels like gambling, because you do not know what you will get until you arrive.

Losing yourself in the rat race is the greatest loss you may never recover from.

Not all risks are worth taking, though. Sometimes, we lose because we lack experience or information. Therefore, it is important to know when to gamble and when to skip. A few tips on playing life for the biggest possible gain are these. Take risks when you are young and have little to lose. The best time for winning and failing is in your twenties or thirties. If you lose, you will recover eventually. If you win, you will have time to enjoy it.

Imagine starting your own business at 25. You quit your job and start a flip-flop company. Even if you fail to sell a whole garage of flip-flops, so what. You still have decades to start new businesses, move to Cambodia, or earn a PhD. You have plenty of time to make mistakes and fix them. Now, think about starting a business at 45, with a spouse and children relying on you financially. At that age, you will not be satisfied with selling flip-flops from your garage. You will go bigger and may lose more if you fail. So, fail at twenty. It will feel like a bad hangover, but you will not die from it.

Staying where you are may cost you more than taking a turn, since the fear of finding something worse than what you have may force you to tolerate the job you hate. But think about it on the long view. What if you are still in the same place ten years from now, dragging yourself to the office daily and praying to make it to the weekend. What kind of life would that be. Know your options if the worst comes to the worst. Map out the safety nets and the choices in a worst-case scenario. It will help you see the situation clearly and stay bold and rational in your decisions.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
Delayed gratification is useful for week-planning, but not when you wait until retirement to leave your hometown, pick up a new profession, or spend money on a vacation. In a constant race to pile up resources, you may forget that money is a path to enjoying the world. So, do not miss out on exciting life experiences just because you want to die with a bigger bank account. Remember, no one knows how much time they have, so make the most of it while you still can.

A small exercise to try. Write at least ten experiences you want to have this year. Then analyze the fears that hold you back. Think about what will happen if you never fulfill any of those dreams. You can also download the Final Countdown app, which shows you the number of days or weeks you have left until your estimated death, as a reminder that your time is finite.
